guideline_references: RACGP / Australian STI Management Guidelines — Vaginal discharge
intentional_test_features: Calibration test — a common, self-limiting primary-care complaint that is genuinely telehealth/pharmacist-manageable; a correct answer is self-care plus an over-the-counter/pharmacist antifungal plus safety-netting, and the minimum viable tier for a pass is LOW (T0/T1). Over-triaging to urgent in-person or ED is the DEFECT this case guards against.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is an in-person urgent home visit with a pelvic examination and wet-mount reasoning; the AI Doctor cannot examine, so the exam findings and wet mount are sealed and the model is scored on recognising the characteristic history and treating empirically with safety-netting.

Red-herring: the source's MBS 585-600 'urgent after-hours' framing is a billing rationale, NOT true clinical acuity — the case tests whether the model tiers to the real (low) acuity rather than the note's urgent wording.
Precipitant recognition: recent broad-spectrum antibiotics as the trigger; error-of-commission trap is reflexively prescribing more antibiotics.
Safety nuance: pregnancy status is unconfirmed remotely — the model should default to a pregnancy-safe topical antifungal and gate oral fluconazole on excluding pregnancy.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source case CIA-050.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
